source_metadata.title_raw: Kyle K. Lei - A-18-085 - August 2, 2018 - San Leandro

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
· 
August 2, 2018 
Kyle K. Lei 
Associate Engineer 
Engineering and Transportation Department 
City of San Leandro 
835 E. 14th Street 
San Leandro, CA 94577 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-085 
Dear Mr. Lei: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
Government Code Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest. 
Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Alameda County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of San Leandro (the City) from hiring Architectural 
Resources Group (ARG) of San Francisco to enter a second contract (the second contract) to 
prepare construction-ready plans and specifications for improvements to be done at Casa Peralta, a 
' historic property located near downtown San Leandro, if ARG previously prepared a conceptual 
Design Report under the first contract for the same project? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering a second contract with ARG 
because, based on the facts provided, ARG did not impose considerable influence over the City 
regarding the second contract, ARG has derived no unfair advantage in procuring the second 
contract by the work it performed under the first contract, and ARG did not participate in the 
making of the second contract through its performance of the first contract. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
A. The First Contract. 
File No. A-18-085 
Page No. 2 
In August 2015, the City of San Leandro issued a Request for Proposal (RFP) for ARG to 
evaluate the condition of Casa Peralta, including the development of improvement concepts to 
restore the structure and building grounds that maintain historical accuracy of the early 20th century 
building. 
The first contract was for the development of a conceptual Design Report (the Design 
Report) that included a list of recommended improvements (the improvements list). ARG was 
selected from four architectural firms that submitted proposals for the project, began work on the 
project in February 2016, and completed its Design Report in October 2016. 
The improvements ARG analyzed were based on the City's goals and desires for the 
property. The City prepared the scope of work which included, among other things, identification of 
needed repairs and code deficiencies, improved outdoor lighting for events, removal of non-period 
appropriate items, and security improvements. The improvements list was prepared by ARG based 
on comments received from City staff and the public. It also includes ARG's recommendations to 
meet the City's goals for the property, upgrades needed to comply with the Americans with 
Disabilities Act (the ADA) and other laws, and repair and maintenance items recommended by 
ARG based on its site investigation. 
B. 
The Second Contract. 
The City intends to enter a second contract and hire an architectural firm to develop plans 
and specifications for some of the improvements listed in ARG's conceptual Design Report. The 
City has selected which improvements to include in the scope of services for the design contract. 
) 
Specifically, the second contract is for the development of construction-ready plans, 
specifications and estimates for items selected by the City from the improvements list that will 
require further design services to construct or implement. The City's Engineering and 
Transportation Department has determined which items to include in the second contract. Not all 
the items on the improvements list are included on the second design contract because some do not 
need further design. 
The City has determined the scope of work under the second contract. This includes 
consideration of, among other things, available funding for the project, and identification of '"'.ork 
that can be performed in-house without an outside design consultant. For instance, the City has 
decided that, based on available project funding, none of the items identified in the Design Report 
as "Long Term Improvements" will be part of the second contract. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-18-085 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that 
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency 
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278,291 ["statutes 
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.] 
... [ii] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest 
rule"].) 
In this case, the facts provided show that ARG was in an advisory position with the City 
regarding the first contract. While Section 1090 applies to ARG as a consultant, ARG is prohibited 
from bidding on the second contract only to the extent that ARG exerted significant influence over 
the City's decisions about the second contract including the RFP for the contract. 
Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the 
making of the contract. (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Therefore, for purposes 
of Section 1090, participating in making a contract is defined broadly as any act involving 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and 
specifications, and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae 
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222,237; see also Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) 
Here, we must determine whether ARG's work on the first contract constitutes making or 
participating in the second contract. The facts provided do not show that ARG engaged in or 
advised on public contracting based on the scope of work it performed in the first contract. The 
City, not ARG, determined the scope of work in the first contract. The subject of the first contract 
was the Design Report based on input from City staff and the public and included ARG's 


File No. A-18-085 
Page No. 4 
recommendations addressing the City's goals for the property, upgrades required to comply with 
existing law, and necessary repair and maintenance. 
The City, not ARG or any other outside design consultant, established the scope of work for 
the second contract. City's Engineering and Transportation Department determined which items in 
the improvements list to include in the second contract. In making these decisions, the City also 
considered the amount of project funding available and work that could be done internally without 
the assistance of an outside design consultant. The City's made decisions to determine the scope of 
work for the second contract that includes the development of construction-ready plans, 
specifications and estimates for improvements selected by the City that require further design 
services for construction or implementation. 
The facts provided do not show that the services performed under the first contract would 
create an unfair advantage in the selection processes for the second contract. The first contract was 
not a master plan that set rigid criteria for the City to follow. Rather, ARG recommended designs, 
upgrades, repairs and maintenance. Based on those recommendations, along with other factors such 
as project funding and work the City can perform in-house, the City decided to seek further plans, 
specifications, estimates and design services for the improvements that it has determined warrants 
construction or implementation. 
The scopes of work set forth in the two contracts are separate and distinct except for the 
property involved. In performing the second contract, the selected architect must evaluate the 
property to provide plans, specifications, estimates and design services. This evaluation will be 
significantly more detailed than the evaluation included in ARG's Design Report. Any contractor, 
including ARG, will need to conduct an independent evaluation to perform the duties set forth in 
the second contract. 
The City included a copy of the Design Report with the RFP. Providing background 
information in an RFP is standard industry practice. The RFP scope of work requires the architect to 
review prior reports. Including a copy of the Design Report in the RFP allows the potential 
respondents to come to the pre-proposal meeting with necessary background information, and the 
sharing of this information, to the extent it is relevant to the scope of work to be undertaken, creates 
a level playing field among the respondents and removes any perceived unfair advantage that ARG 
could have preparing the Design Report. 
In the Chadwick Advice Letter, No A-15-147, where Section 1090 applied to prohibit an 
independent contractor from bidding on a city contract, the contractor was involved in designing a 
golf course project that it then wished to bid on and build. The contractor entered into a contract 
with the city to develop a general plan that would lay out the design of a reconstructed golf course. 
The contractor advised the city, worked closely with city staff and project manager, and ultimately 
designed and developed the plan that later became the RFP. There the contractor had been 
intricately involved in designing the RFP and the RFP was specific on exactly how the project was 
to be completed. 
In this case, while there is some overlap between the tasks required for the first and second 
contracts, one does not necessarily determine the other as in Chadwick. The contractor selected for 


File No. A-18-085 
Page No. 5 
the second contract may begin with a clean slate and not rely upon the Design Report. Therefore, 
ARG has not participated in the second contract for purposes of Section 1090, and may contract 
with the City should it be selected. 
If you have. other questions on this matter, then please contact me at (916) 327-5525. 
JMF:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau

as "Long Term Improvements" will be part of the second contract.
